title: Inter-harmonic ratio structure and saturation of Bernstein modes in graphene
abstract: Bernstein modes (BM) in graphene are finite-wavevector magnetoplasmons excited by contact near fields, whereas ordinary cyclotron resonance (CR) probes $q\approx0$. We derive the BM peak absorption in the quasiclassical ballistic regime and show that it factorizes into a launch spectrum, Bernstein-mode splitting, turning-point enhancement, and residual dielectric-response factor. At fixed excitation frequency, BM overtones ($n\ge2$) are sampled, to leading order, at the same momentum $q\simeqω/v_F$. Smooth launch and screening factors therefore cancel in inter-harmonic peak ratios, yielding $I_n/I_m\simeq m/n$, modified by linewidth corrections and one residual response ratio for each harmonic pair. In smooth-launcher synthetic tests, noisy full-$q$ spectra recover the residual ratio within errors: moderate launcher/dielectric misspecification within this benchmark family shifts it by only $\sim\!1$--$2\%$, whereas linewidth assumptions shift it by $\sim\!10$--$30\%$. The same factorization connects low-power amplitudes to nonlinear saturation. If BM harmonics share the same cooling region and bolometric readout, the low-power slope times onset intensity is harmonic independent, while BM and CR power sweeps obey distinct normalized saturation curves with linewidth scalings $Γ^{-1/2}$ and $Γ^{-1}$.
summary: This paper provides a theoretical framework to separate intrinsic electronic response from experimental artifacts in graphene's Bernstein modes. It establishes a universal scaling law for harmonic intensity ratios and predicts distinct power-dependent saturation behaviors for Bernstein modes versus cyclotron resonance.
main_problem: The paper aims to develop a robust protocol for decomposing Bernstein mode (BM) absorption in graphene into intrinsic dielectric properties and device-specific effects, while also establishing a link between linear absorption and nonlinear saturation.
main_result: The authors derive a factorization theorem for BM peak absorption and show that inter-harmonic ratios follow a parameter-free m/n baseline, which is robust against launcher/dielectric errors but sensitive to linewidth assumptions. They also demonstrate that BM and cyclotron resonance (CR) follow distinct nonlinear saturation scaling laws.
method: The study uses kinetic plasma theory, a hot-electron energy-balance model, and asymptotic analysis (Debye-Olfer/Airy functions) alongside synthetic recovery tests using Monte-Carlo simulations and full q-integral benchmarks.
model_or_system: The physical system is graphene in the quasiclassical, ballistic, and degenerate regime under a perpendicular magnetic field, specifically focusing on finite-wavevector Bernstein modes excited via near-field metallic contacts.
key_observables: BM peak absorption amplitudes, inter-harmonic intensity ratios (In/Im), linewidths (Gamma), and nonlinear saturation curves.
important_parameters: Cyclotron frequency (omega_c), cyclotron mass (m_c), wavevector (q approx omega/v_F), linewidth exponent (s_BM = 1/2), and the bolometric transfer coefficient (beta).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the quasiclassical regime (neglecting Landau quantization), a ballistic transport regime, and that the launcher/dielectric prefactors vary slowly across the resonance window.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the experimental geometry; Figure 2 shows numerical agreement between the factorized formula and full q-integrals; Figure 3 illustrates kinematic weight functions; Figure 5 displays normalized nonlinear saturation master curves; Figure 6 shows a stress test for model misspecification.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the BM absorption factorization, moves to the derivation of universal inter-harmonic ratios and their robustness, introduces a protocol for extracting intrinsic residues, extends the theory to nonlinear power-dependent saturation, and concludes with validation via synthetic stress tests.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Bernstein-mode geometry. The 2D plasmon branch (black) rises with in-plane wavevector q and hybridizes with cyclotron harmonics ω = nωc (gray dashed), producing avoided crossings. The lower Bernstein branch (blue) arches toward nωc from below and flattens at a turning point (∂qωBM = 0, red dot), where the group velocity vanishes and absorption is strongly enhanced [20]; the upper branch emerges above nωc. Both branches possess turning points; the single-pole resonant form Eq. (2) describes the upper branch (ω = nωc + Ωn > nωc), but the splitting Ωn, the curvature scaling, and the resulting n−1 ordering of peak heights are common to both. Ordinary far-field cyclotron resonance probes...

FIG. 2. Numerical benchmark of the factorized peak formula against the full q-integral (1) with the quasiclassical nonlocal dielectric function. (a) Absorbed power vs. B/BCR (normal- ized to the n = 2 peak): filled circles mark full-integral peaks, open squares mark the factorized predictions (offset slightly in B for visibility). (b) Peak heights normalized to n = 2: the factorized approximation agrees with the full integral to +2% (n = 3) and −3% (n = 4). Parameters: f = 2.54 THz, αg = 0.51, kF = 3.1 × 108 m−1, Γ/ωc = 0.03, unscreened Coulomb, model launcher (A15).

FIG. 3. The function hn(ζ) = (n/ζ)2J2 n(ζ) entering the split- ting Ωn [Eq. (3)], for n = 2, 3, 4. Filled circles mark the Bessel peaks ζ(h) n (to be distinguished from the physical turning point ζ∗ n of the full Ωn, which is shifted outward by the q-slope of ω2 p; see Table III); the n−2/3 decay of the peak heights and the convergence of ζ(h) n /n toward a common value are both visible. The curvature at the peak controls Kn (Sec. III), and the inter-harmonic peak shift controls the pairwise launcher correction (Sec. III).

FIG. 4. Synthetic recovery test for the factorization protocol. (a) Absorption profiles Pn(∆ω) for n = 2, 3, 4 generated by di- rect q-quadrature of Eq. (1) with the dielectric function Eq. (2), the Coulomb kernel Eq. (A3), and the launcher Eq. (A15); solid lines, noiseless; dots, one 3% per-point noise realization. (b) Decomposition of the (2, 3) raw amplitude ratio A2/A3 (left bar of each pair: noiseless target from the profile; right bar: 400-realization median with 1σ Monte-Carlo error) into the baseline B(0) 23 = 1.54, the launcher/screening correction Cgeom 23 = 0.95, the raw-ratio linewidth factor p

FIG. 5. Normalized saturation master curves in the low-TL limit for clean graphene (δ = 4), with mock power-sweep traces generated from the energy-balance model overlaid (symbols). The plotted quantity is the normalized peak amplitude aν = αν(Iinc)/αν,0 = ∆Rν(Iinc)/[AνIinc] versus the scaled intensity Iinc/Ifit 0,ν, where Ifit 0,ν is fitted per trace as the one-parameter horizontal rescaling. Mock traces are generated by solving the energy balance Eq. (31) for X(Iinc) with r = TL/T∗,ν = 0.2, adding 4% multiplicative noise, and fitting Aν on the lowest- power linear portion.
